title: Apply: The Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory
description: Apply your understanding of the Divine Rights of Kings and Social Contract Theory in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Principles of the American Revolution: The Ideas and Events That Led to Change
The Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory
Apply: The Divine Right of Kings and Social Contract Theory

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from the *The Social Contract* (1762) by Jean-Jacques Rousseau.**

This formula shows us that the act of association comprises a mutual undertaking between the public and the individuals, and that each individual, in making a contract, as we may say, with himself, is bound in a double capacity; as a member of the Sovereign he is bound to the individuals, and as a member of the State to the Sovereign. But the [[☃ definition 1]] of civil right, that no one is bound by undertakings made to himself, does not apply in this case; for there is a great difference between [[☃ definition 2]] an obligation to yourself and incurring one to a whole of which you form a part.

**What argument does Rousseau make in this excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When people form a society, they take on obligations to each other and to the larger community.
When people form a government, they agree never to challenge the authority of that government.
When people choose a leader, that person must always listen to the will of the people.
When people choose a government, they agree to give up all of their natural rights for the government to function.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from the *The Social Contract* (1762) by Jean-Jacques Rousseau.**

This formula shows us that the act of association comprises a mutual undertaking between the public and the individuals, and that each individual, in making a contract, as we may say, with himself, is bound in a double capacity; as a member of the Sovereign he is bound to the individuals, and as a member of the State to the Sovereign. But the [[☃ definition 1]] of civil right, that no one is bound by undertakings made to himself, does not apply in this case; for there is a great difference between [[☃ definition 2]] an obligation to yourself and incurring one to a whole of which you form a part.

**Which action is an example of the relationship described in the excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A ruler orders men in her kingdom to protect the borders against invaders.
A ruler makes a law that only people in his family can rule his kingdom.
A ruler promises to protect the people in his kingdom in exchange for taxes.
A ruler negotiates a treaty with a neighboring kingdom to prevent war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from the *The Social Contract* (1762) by Jean-Jacques Rousseau.**

Since no man has a natural authority over his fellow, and force creates no right, we must conclude that [[☃ definition 1]] form the basis of all legitimate authority among men.


**Which statement best summarizes Rousseau’s argument?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: rules or practices that people generally agree to

choices.content: Only kings have the right to rule, as they are chosen by God.
Only the consent of citizens can authorize the powers of a government.
Only military force can prove the right of a ruler to control a government.
Only democracies, where people choose their leaders, are legitimate.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from the *The Social Contract* (1762) by Jean-Jacques Rousseau.**

Since no man has a natural authority over his fellow, and force creates no right, we must conclude that [[☃ definition 1]] form the basis of all legitimate authority among men.


**Which idea does Rousseau challenge in this excerpt?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: rules or practices that people generally agree to

choices.content: The idea that people are born with the right to life, liberty, and property
The idea that citizens can change a government that they do not agree with
The idea that citizens must follow a government and not exist in disorder
The idea that God chooses who becomes the head of a government

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the *Discourses Concerning Government* (1763) by Algernon Sidney.**

[M]en are generally inclined to follow the examples of those in power, but from a most certain conclusion, that he who breaks his part of a contract, cannot without the utmost [[☃ definition 1]] and [[☃ definition 2]] expect the performance of the other; nothing being more known amongst men, than, that all contracts are of such mutual obligation, that he who fails of his part discharges the other. If this be so between man and man, it must needs be so between one and many millions of men. . .

**What argument does Algernon Sidney make in this excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: That if one person breaks the promises in a contract, the other person can do the same
That kings are chosen by and get their power from God, and they are therefore meant to rule harshly
That entering into a contract with a king is pointless, as kings will always lie and take advantage of you
That people should always follow the example of those in power, since powerful people are usually right

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the *Discourses Concerning Government* (1763) by Algernon Sidney.**

[M]en are generally inclined to follow the examples of those in power, but from a most certain conclusion, that he who breaks his part of a contract, cannot without the utmost [[☃ definition 1]] and [[☃ definition 2]] expect the performance of the other; nothing being more known amongst men, than, that all contracts are of such mutual obligation, that he who fails of his part discharges the other. If this be so between man and man, it must needs be so between one and many millions of men. . .

**The arguments in the passage are most related to which idea?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Each person has a set of rights that they are born with and that cannot be taken away.
The government’s authority comes from the agreement of its citizens.
The right to rule is granted by a divine power and cannot be challenged.
Each person is responsible for pursuing a virtuous life.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The Second Treatise of Government* (1689) by John Locke.**
 
First, It is not, nor can possibly be absolutely [[☃ definition 1]] over the lives and fortunes of the people: for it being but the joint power of every member of the society given up to that person, or assembly, which is legislator; it can be no more than those persons had in a state of nature before they entered into society, and gave up to the community: for no body can transfer to another more power than he has in himself; and no body has an absolute arbitrary power over himself, or over any other, to destroy his own life, or take away the life or property of another. 

A man, as has been proved, cannot subject himself to the arbitrary power of another; and having in the state of nature no arbitrary power over the life, liberty, or possession of another, but only so much as the law of nature gave him for the preservation of himself, and the rest of mankind; this is all he doth, or can give up to the commonwealth, and by it to the legislative power, so that the legislative can have no more than this. Their power, in the utmost bounds of it, is limited to the public good of the society. 

**According to Locke, what is the purpose of a government?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To secure as much power as possible
To maintain order and prevent disorder
To ensure that the majority’s voice is heard
To protect citizens’ rights and promote the general welfare

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The Second Treatise of Government* (1689) by John Locke.**
 
First, It is not, nor can possibly be absolutely [[☃ definition 1]] over the lives and fortunes of the people: for it being but the joint power of every member of the society given up to that person, or assembly, which is legislator; it can be no more than those persons had in a state of nature before they entered into society, and gave up to the community: for no body can transfer to another more power than he has in himself; and no body has an absolute arbitrary power over himself, or over any other, to destroy his own life, or take away the life or property of another. 

A man, as has been proved, cannot subject himself to the arbitrary power of another; and having in the state of nature no arbitrary power over the life, liberty, or possession of another, but only so much as the law of nature gave him for the preservation of himself, and the rest of mankind; this is all he doth, or can give up to the commonwealth, and by it to the legislative power, so that the legislative can have no more than this. Their power, in the utmost bounds of it, is limited to the public good of the society. 

**How do the ideas in this passage relate to social contract theory?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The passage suggests that governments only work when people agree to give up all of their power for the good of the community.
The passage suggests that governments are most effective when the people have a voice in governmental decision-making.
The passage suggests that governments are limited to serving the public good, and if they fail that, citizens have the right to dismantle governments.
The passage suggests that governments are only legitimate when they have unlimited power over the people in their domain.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the *Discourses Concerning Government* (1698) by Algernon Sidney.**

If any man ask how Nations come to have the power of doing these things, I answer, that Liberty being only an [[☃ definition 1]] from the [[☃ definition 2]] of another, the question ought not to be, how a Nation can come to be free, but how a man comes to have a dominion over it; for till the right of dominion be proved and justified, liberty [[☃ definition 3]] as arising from the nature and being of a man. 

**Which statement best summarizes Sidney’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: freedom from some obligation or responsibility

choices.content: The right to rule is legitimate, as it is given by a higher power.
A government’s power comes from the voluntary surrender of individual liberties.
Every person is born with freedom and not under the control of another.
A good government does not violate people’s liberty, as liberty is a natural right.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the *Discourses Concerning Government* (1698) by Algernon Sidney.**

If any man ask how Nations come to have the power of doing these things, I answer, that Liberty being only an [[☃ definition 1]] from the [[☃ definition 2]] of another, the question ought not to be, how a Nation can come to be free, but how a man comes to have a dominion over it; for till the right of dominion be proved and justified, liberty [[☃ definition 3]] as arising from the nature and being of a man. 

**The ideas in the excerpt most directly conflict with what belief commonly held in the seventeenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: freedom from some obligation or responsibility

choices.content: Governments are responsible for distributing property equally to citizens.
A democracy is the best form of government, as the people choose their leaders.
People have the responsibility to replace unjust governments.
Kings are chosen directly by God to rule over people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The Second Treatise of Government* (1689) by John Locke.**

The [[☃ definition 1]] of Law is not to abolish or restrain, but to preserve and enlarge Freedom: For in all the states of created beings capable of Laws, where there is no Law, there is no Freedom. For Liberty is to be free from restraint and violence from others, which cannot be, where there is no Law: But Freedom is not, as we are told, A Liberty for every Man to do what he [[☃ definition 2]]: (For who could be free, when every other Man’s [[☃ definition 3]] might [[☃ definition 4]] over him?) But a Liberty to [[☃ definition 5]], and order, as he [[☃ definition 6]], his Person, Actions, Possessions, and his whole Property, within the Allowance of those Laws under which he is; and therein not to be subject to the [[☃ definition 7]] Will of another, but freely follow his own.

**Which statement best summarizes Locke’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: feelings or mental state at a particular time
based on personal preference, not reason

choices.content: To have a well-functioning government that protects citizens, citizens must give up some of their rights.
The government’s purpose is not to pass laws to restrict freedom, but to protect the freedoms every person is born with.
The people can change or restrict the government’s powers only if they are granted that authority in their constitution.
True freedom exists only when there are no laws and no government to restrict people’s rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The Second Treatise of Government* (1689) by John Locke.**

The [[☃ definition 1]] of Law is not to abolish or restrain, but to preserve and enlarge Freedom: For in all the states of created beings capable of Laws, where there is no Law, there is no Freedom. For Liberty is to be free from restraint and violence from others, which cannot be, where there is no Law: But Freedom is not, as we are told, A Liberty for every Man to do what he [[☃ definition 2]]: (For who could be free, when every other Man’s [[☃ definition 3]] might [[☃ definition 4]] over him?) But a Liberty to [[☃ definition 5]], and order, as he [[☃ definition 6]], his Person, Actions, Possessions, and his whole Property, within the Allowance of those Laws under which he is; and therein not to be subject to the [[☃ definition 7]] Will of another, but freely follow his own.

**This excerpt can be used as evidence to support which concept?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: feelings or mental state at a particular time
based on personal preference, not reason

choices.content: Limited government
Divine right of kings
The right to revolution
The rule of law
